coredata.dc:description: Hydroquinone (HQ) and catechol (CC) are toxic isomers with similar structures that often coexist, leading to interference during detection. In this study, we employed a controllable selenization strategy to construct V2CTx MXene-based multiphase electrode materials (VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene) for the simultaneous detection of HQ and CC. Electrochemical tests revealed VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene modified electrode exhibited low electrochemical impedance, a large active surface area, and excellent electrocatalytic performance. The sensor demonstrated linear detection ranges for HQ and CC from 0.5 to 600 μM, with limits of detection 0.144 μM and 0.131 μM, respectively. Additionally, it exhibited excellent repeatability, reproducibility, selectivity, and stability. The practical applicability of the sensor was confirmed through testing in drinking water and beverages, showing satisfactory recovery rates. This work expands the application of V2CTx MXene in the field of electrochemical sensing and offers new insights for food safety monitoring.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-c) SEM images of L-V2CTx MXene, CQDs@MXene, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene; (d-e) TEM images of L-V2CTx MXene, CQDs@MXene, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene; SEM image (g) and corresponding elemental mappings of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene, C (h), N (i), O (j), V (k), and Se (l).

(a) XRD patterns of CQDs, L-V2CTx MXene, CQDs@MXene and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene; (b) HRTEM image of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene; (c-e) IFFT images of areas; (f) XPS survey spectra for L-V2CTx MXene, CQDs@MXene and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene, and high resolution XPS spectra of V 2p (g), and O 1 s (h).

(a) CVs and (b) EIS spectra of bare/GCE, L-V2CTx MXene/GCE, CQDs@MXene/GCE, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE in a 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- a solution containing 0.1 M KCl at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1; (c) the equivalent circuit; (d-f) CVs of the L- V2CTx MXene, CQDs@MXene, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene in a 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- solution containing 0.1 M KCl at various scan rates; (g) The plots of Ipa vs the square root of the scan rate of L-V2CTx MXene/GCE, CQDs@MXene/GCE, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE; (h) CVs of bare GCE, L-V2CTx MXene/GCE, CQDs@MXene/GCE, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE with 100 μM CC and 100 μM HQ in 0.1 M PBS; (i) Comprehensive electrocatalytic performance comparison of bare GCE, L-V2CTx MXene/GCE, CQDs@MXene/GCE, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE.

(a) DPV curves of 100 μM HQ and CC at VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE in 0.1 M PBS with different pH values (6.0–8.0); (b, c) Plots of anodic peak current (I pa) and peak potential (E pa) versus pH values for HQ and CC, respectively; (d) CV curves of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE in 0.1 M PBS containing 100 μM HQ and CC; (e) Correlation curve of peak current vs square root of scan rate; (f) Corresponding linear relationship between the oxidation potentials of HQ and CC and the logarithm of the scan rate; (g,h) Proposed reaction mechanisms for the electrochemical redox of HQ and CC.

(a-c) DPV curves of varying concentrations of HQ, CC, and simultaneous addition of HQ and CC in 0.1 M PBS using VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE; (d-f) The relationship between the analyte concentrations and the corresponding response current; (g) A comparative analysis of the electrochemical sensors for determination of HQ and CC.

(a) DPV response of HQ and CC in the presence of 10-fold interference compounds on the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE; (b) Selectivity of the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE towards HQ and CC; (c) Repeatability of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE; (d) Reproducibility of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE; (e) Stability of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE.
Schematic diagram of (a) the synthesis of materials and (b) the construction of the HQ/CC sensor.

head.ce:title: Controllable selenization strategy for in-situ construction of V2CTx MXene-based multiphase electrode materials for simultaneous detection of hydroquinone and catechol in drinking water and beverages

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Polymeric Composite Materials, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydroquinone (HQ) and catechol (CC) are toxic isomers with similar structures that often coexist, leading to interference during detection. In this study, we employed a controllable selenization strategy to construct V2CTx MXene-based multiphase electrode materials (VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene) for the simultaneous detection of HQ and CC. Electrochemical tests revealed VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene modified electrode exhibited low electrochemical impedance, a large active surface area, and excellent electrocatalytic performance. The sensor demonstrated linear detection ranges for HQ and CC from 0.5 to 600 μM, with limits of detection 0.144 μM and 0.131 μM, respectively. Additionally, it exhibited excellent repeatability, reproducibility, selectivity, and stability. The practical applicability of the sensor was confirmed through testing in drinking water and beverages, showing satisfactory recovery rates. This work expands the application of V2CTx MXene in the field of electrochemical sensing and offers new insights for food safety monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Two common phenolic compounds, hydroquinone (HQ) and catechol (CC), are found in many products and uses, including organic dyes, cosmetics, film production, and some foods. They are easily dissolved in water. It is worth noting that even minimal concentrations of HQ and CC can lead to serious health issues, including cancer, liver function, and respiratory system damage (Iftikhar et al., 2022). Therefore, accurate measurements are crucial for effective water quality monitoring. The similar properties and structures of these diphenyl isomers, along with their coexistence in various environments, pose challenges for their simultaneous, rapid, and sensitive detection. Commonly used detection methods include capillary electrophoresis (Nagaraja et al., 2001), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Kanamori et al., 2015), spectrophotometry (Xie et al., 2006), and electrochemical methods (Niu et al., 2024). The electrochemical method is particularly popular for pollutant analysis due to its rapid and effective detection capabilities. While conventional bio-enzyme-modified materials exhibit good electrocatalytic activity, their high expense and low stability have constrained their application in real-world scenarios (Harraz et al., 2019). Consequently, there is a pressing requirement to create effective and stable non-enzymatic materials capable of simultaneously determining both CC and HQ.

MXene represents a novel class of two-dimensional inorganic compounds characterized by the chemical formula of Mn+1XnTx, where M represents the transition metal, n ranges from 1 to 3, X represents C and/or N, and Tx indicates the presence of surface functional groups (Gopalakrishnan et al., 2024). This material has attracted considerable interest within the field of electrochemical sensing owing to its hydrophilic nature, environmental sustainability, remarkable electrical conductivity, superior energy storage capabilities, extensive specific surface area, and thermal stability (Sun et al., 2025; Zhang, Han, Sun, & Li, 2024). Among the various MXene materials, V2CTx has been particularly noted for its exceptional conductivity and excellent electrochemical performance. Notably, MXenes of the M2XTx type possess an atomic count of 3 per MXene sheet, in contrast to the 5 or 7 found in the M3X2Tx and M4X3Tx structures. This structural characteristic contributes to an increased surface area and a higher density of active sites (Zhang, Han, Sun, Li, Li, & Gui, 2024; Zhu et al., 2021). Additionally, the vanadium atoms in V2CTx MXene exhibit a variety of oxidation states (+2, +3, +4), which enhances the efficiency of the electrocatalytic electron transfer reactions (Yadav et al., 2023). In particular, intercalation with organic solvents and ultrasonic synthesis can produce few-layer two-dimensional V2CTx MXene structures with enhanced surface areas and significantly improved electrochemical performance. However, the limited number of layers in V2CTx MXene leads to an enhancement of van der Waals interactions between the layers. This intensified interaction leads to restacking of the layered structure and reducing the interlayer spacing. This phenomenon diminishes the effective use of the active surface area and significantly impairs their electrochemical performance (Zhang et al., 2022). To optimize the electrochemical characteristics of V2CTx MXene nanosheets, several approaches have been suggested to increase the interlayer spacing of MXene nanosheets. A prevalent method involves the incorporation of an intercalator, which serves to increase the distance between neighboring MXene nanosheets (Zhang, Ma, et al., 2023). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered increasing interest within the domain of electrochemical sensing due to their high compatibility, easy surface modification, and good conductivity (Bao et al., 2023). MXene nanosheets are separated by CQDs to prevent overlap, which can also increase the surface activity of MXene (Wang et al., 2021; Zahedi et al., 2024). Nevertheless, the inclusion of CQDs to improve the interlayer spacing does not completely eliminate the issue of nanosheet overlap because of the extremely well-organized structure of MXene nanosheets (Zhang, Zhang, et al., 2023).

To address the issue of overlapping of MXene nanosheets, recent research has proposed the in-situ synthesis of two-dimensional transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) on the surfaces of MXene is an effective strategy proposed in recent years. For instance, Chen et al. sulfurized Ti3C2Tx MXene and successfully grew TiS2 nanoparticles in situ between MXene sheets, effectively inhibiting the overlap of MXene nanosheets (Chen et al., 2024). Furthermore, TMDs are recognized as promising electrocatalytic materials, especially transition metal selenides, which exhibit superior conductivity, lower formation enthalpy, and comparable crystal structure and polarity characteristics to MXene when contrasted with sulfides (Luo, Guo, He, et al., 2024; Luo, Guo, Qian, et al., 2024; Sha et al., 2022; Zheng et al., 2024). Therefore, the selenization strategy emerges as a more effective approach for enhancing MXene. This method not only addresses the issue of MXene overlap but also improves its electrochemical activity, making it more suitable for developing efficient, sensitive, and stable electrochemical sensors.

Inspired by the above excellent research findings, we employed CQDs intercalation and gas-phase pyrolysis to achieve controlled partial selenization of few-layer V2CTx MXene, resulting in a structurally stable VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene heterostructure designed for simultaneous electrochemical detection of HQ and CC. The morphologies, structural properties, and electrochemical performance of the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene were characterized by extensive material characterization and electrochemical tests. The prepared electrochemical sensor has a large electroactive surface area, wide linear range, and low limit of detection for both HQ and CC. Furthermore, the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene sensor exhibits notable selectivity, exceptional reproducibility, repeatability, and excellent stability, along with satisfactory recovery in the analysis of drinking water and beverages. To the best of our knowledge, there has been a paucity of studies focusing on the application of V2CTx MXene in electrochemical sensors in recent years. This research represents the inaugural report of using V2CTx MXene as a precursor to in situ construct a V2CTx MXene-based multiphase electrode material through a controlled selenization strategy for simultaneous detection of HQ and CC via electrochemical methods.

LiF, tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH), citric acid monohydrate (CA), and selenium power were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai) Company. Ethanol (EtOH), and HCl were purchased from Quan Rui Reagent Co.Ltd. (Liaoning). Urea was purchased from Chemical Reagent Co.Ltd. (Tianjin). V2AlC 400 mesh was purchased from Xinxi Technology Co.Ltd. (Foshan). All chemicals used without further treatment or purification.

The SEM analyses were performed on TESCAN MIRA LMS with Quantax 200 XFlash 6/60 provided by eceshi (www.eceshi.com), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, H-7650, HITACHI, Japan) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; Tecnai G2 F30). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was utilized to analyze the samples, with data collected on a Smart Lab diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8, BRUKER-AXS, Germany). The elemental composition, chemical bonding and chemical valence were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB-MKII, VG Scientific, United Kingdom).

The preparation of L-V2CTx MXene was broadly divided into two steps. The first step involves synthesizing V2CTx through a wet chemical etching method using lithium fluoride and hydrochloric acid. Briefly, 1 g of V2AlC MAX phase was gradually added to a mixture of 2 g of LiF and 20 mL of 6 M HCl mixture solution. The etching reaction was then allowed to proceed for 72 h at a temperature of 90 °C. Following this, the resulting product was washed with deionized water through multiple centrifugation cycles until the pH was close to 7, yielding multilayer V2CTx MXene.

The second step involves delaminating the V2CTx using TMAOH. In this process, 1 g of the freshly etched V2CTx powder (in the wet state) is mixed with 20 mL of a 5 wt% TMAOH solution in water, and the solution was stirred at 400 rpm at room temperature (25 °C) for 6 h. After stirring in the delaminating agent, the intercalated multilayer powder is transferred to a centrifuge tube, and the solution is centrifuged at 8500 rpm for 10 min, discarding the first supernatant. Subsequently, the precipitate was centrifugally washed with anhydrous ethanol three times (3500 rpm, 10 min) and then dispersed into 30 mL of deionized water and sonicated under nitrogen protection for 1 h. After sonication, the supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 3500 rpm, the precipitate was dispersed again in deionized water and the supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 3500 rpm, and the procedure was repeated until the supernatant was clear. Finally, the collected supernatant was centrifuged again at 8500 rpm for 10 min, and the resulting precipitate was vacuum dried at 60 °C for 12 h to produce a thinner layer of V2CTx MXene powder, referred to L-V2CTx MXene.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA) (5.0 g) and urea (1.0 g) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, respectively. The prepared solution was subsequently transferred to a PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Following the heating process, the autoclave was allowed to cool to ambient temperature (25 °C), after which the resulting CQD dispersion was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 50 min to remove any residue. Then, the light brown supernatant was dialyzed using a 7500 kD molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) membrane for 16 h. The solution obtained by dialysis was collected as CQDs solution.

0.5 g of L-V2CTx MXene was dissolved in 15 mL of CQDs solution and underwent ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Following this, the resulting mixture was put in an autoclave and heated to 160 °C for 3 h. After allowing the mixture to cool naturally to ambient temperature, the precipitate was isolated through centrifugation at 8500 rpm for 10 min. The precipitate underwent a purification process, being rinsed five times with deionized water, and then drying for 6 h at 80 °C in a vacuum drying apparatus. The final dried precipitate was collected and designated as CQDs@MXene.

A mixture of 0.2 g of CQDs@MXene and 0.4 g of Se powder was milled for ten minutes. Following that, the resulting mixture was annealed for 2 h at 550 °C with a heating rate of 2 °C per minute in an Ar environment. After cooling to ambient temperature, VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene nanohybrids were obtained. The overall steps are shown in Scheme 1 (a).

Electrochemical measurements were taken using a CHI electrochemical potentiostat (CHI 760E, CH Instruments, China). The VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE was employed as the working electrode, while Ag/AgCl and Pt wires served as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) as a highly sensitive technique was employed for the electrochemical measurements with the following parameters: an amplitude of 100 mV, an incremental potential of 4 mV, a pulse period of 0.5 s, and a pulse width of 0.05 s.

As shown in Scheme 1 (b), the glassy carbon electrodes (GCEs) were first polished with 0.3 μm and 0.05 μm alumina pastes and then washed continuously with deionized water. Subsequently, 2 mg of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene was dispersed in 0.1 mL of deionized water, and 10 μL of this dispersion was dropped on the surface of the GCE. Then the modified electrode was placed in the oven at 50 °C for 10 min, resulting in the designation of the electrode as VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE.

In this study, drinking water and beverages were chosen as real samples for detecting HQ and CC, respectively. The drinking water and beverages were purchased from local supermarkets. The drinking water could be tested directly without any special treatment, while the beverages required centrifugation and a 1:10 dilution with deionized water before testing.

The morphologies and microstructures of CQDs, L-V2CTx MXene, CQDs@MXene, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE were characterized. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, the SEM image of L-V2CTx MXene reveals a layered structure with less lamellar stacking. The 2D morphology of an individual L-V2CTx MXene nanosheet is validated by the TEM image presented in Fig. 1d, which displays a smooth surface (Yu et al., 2024). Furthermore, Fig. 1b depicts the SEM image of CQDs@MXene, indicating a notable increase in the overall layer spacing compared to L-V2CTx MXene, attributed to the incorporation of CQDs within the interlayers of L-V2CTx MXene. The morphology and internal interlayer structure of the CQDs@MXene are further studied by TEM. Fig. S1 shows dispersed CQDs with a size of 5–10 nm, while Fig. 1e illustrates these small-sized CQDs anchored within the layer spacing of L-V2CTx MXene through hydrothermal processing (an orange circle marks CQDs) (Hu et al., 2024; Tuna et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2021). Fig. 1c reveals that the surface selenization does not damage the overall sheet layer of V2CTx MXene. Though the V2CTx surface becomes rough due to the formation of dense VSe2 nanosheets, as confirmed by the TEM image in Fig. 1 f. The VSe2 nanosheets, measuring between 50 and 100 nm, are uniformly distributed across the surface of the MXene, resulting in the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene nanohybrid material exhibiting an increased active surface area. This enhancement suggests a potential improvement in the sensing performance of HQ and CC. Fig. 1(g-l) provides scanning SEM images along with corresponding elemental mappings of the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene. The uniform distribution of vanadium within the nanoflakes further substantiates that the lamellar structure of V2CTx was not disrupted during the surface selenization process, and Se signals appear in the bright regions of the nanosheets, indicating that the VSe2 nanosheets are indeed grown on the V2CTx surface. The elemental distribution agrees with the previously stated SEM and TEM findings.

The crystal structures and phase characteristics of CQDs, L-V2CTx MXene, CQDs@ MXene, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene were examined, as illustrated in Fig. 2a. The CQDs exhibit an amorphous phase, evident from the broad diffraction peaks between 15°-35°, which is consistent with the XRD spectra of graphite (JCPDS No. 89–8487) (Wang et al., 2023). The XRD pattern of L-V2CTx MXene, synthesized via HCl/LiF etching, TMAOH intercalation, and shock ultrasonication, shows left-shifted from 13.5° to 9.0° for the (002) peak of L-V2CTx MXene compared to V2AlC (JCPDS No. 29–0101), with a notable reduction in the intensity of other V2AlC peaks. The findings are in alignment with earlier studies, and these results together with SEM and TEM data indicate the successful preparation of L-V2CTx MXene (Naguib et al., 2013; VahidMohammadi et al., 2017). Analysis of the XRD results for CQDs@MXene shows a broader carbon peak in the range of 15° to 35°, suggesting the incorporation of CQDs into the interlayers of MXene. Furthermore, a significant leftward shift of the (002) peak was observed. This alteration in peak position, along with SEM findings, confirms that the intercalation of CQDs has increased the interlayer spacing of V2CTx MXene.

Following the process of surface selenization, the emergence of new diffraction peaks at 14.5°, 29.2°, 34.1°, 42.9°, 55.0°, and 65.9° were observed, which are associated with the (001), (002), (011), (102), (103), and (201) planes of VSe2 (JCPDS No. 89–1641), respectively, confirming the successful selenization of V2CTx MXene (Li et al., 2023; Wang, Wang, et al., 2023). Additionally, new peaks at 24.3°, 32.9°, 38.5°, 41.2°, 49.8°, 53.9°, and 58.3° correspond to (012), (104), (006), (113), (024), (116), and (122) planes of V2O3 (JCPDS No. 34–0187) (Li et al., 2024). The formation of V2O3 likely results from the replacement of functional groups with hydroxyl groups during the hydrothermal reaction for the synthesis of CQDs@MXene, followed by the formation of V2O3 in the high-temperature environment of selenization. Furthermore, the disappearance of the carbon peak at 26.3° and the right shift of the (002) peak indicates the CQDs in the interlayer of V2CTx MXene are removed after selenization. This removal might be due to the fact that during the high-temperature reaction of selenization, the CQDs adsorbed on the surface of the MXene sheet serve to protect certain vanadium atoms from selenization. Consequently, these vanadium atoms remain unreacted, leading to the formation of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene. To validate this hypothesis, we conducted direct selenization and XRD analyses on L-V2CTx MXene without CQDs intercalation. The results indicate that V2O3 peaks are nearly absent and the (002) peak for V2CTx MXene completely vanishes after selenization (Fig. S2). This suggests that the V2CTx MXene undergoes nearly complete selenization in the absence of CQDs intercalation. Therefore, our strategy of intercalating CQDs can be described as a “kill two birds with one stone” approach. This method not only expands the interlayer spacing of L-V2CTx MXene but also protects it during the selenization process, effectively controlling its partial transformation into a heterogeneous structure consisting of VSe2, V2O3, and V2CTx MXene.

Fig. 2d and e present the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images along with their respective inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) images for the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene. The observed lattice fringes with spacings of 0.245 nm, 0.17 nm, and 0.264 nm, which correspond to the (101), (116), and (011) planes of V2CTx, V2O3, and VSe2, respectively (Wei et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2018). These findings corroborate the composition of the nanohybrids, comprising VSe2, V2O3, and V2CTx, in alignment with the results obtained from XRD analysis. In addition, there is a clear grain boundary between VSe2, V2O3, and V2CTx, as shown by the yellow dashed line in Fig. 2b, which implies the chemical growth of VSe2 and V2O3 on V2CTx.

Changes in elemental valence states after selenization were further analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2f displays XPS survey spectra for L-V2CTx MXene, CQDs@MXene, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene. The spectrum of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene shows a characteristic Se peak at 55 eV, which is not present in the spectra of L-V2CTx MXene and CQDs@MXene, suggesting the successful selenization of CQDs@MXene.

Fig. 2g displays the high-resolution XPS spectra of V 2p. Three distinct pairs of peaks are observed at 523.8/516.2 eV, 522.0/514.4 eV, and 520.7/513.1 eV, corresponding to the V 2p1/2/2p3/2 orbitals of V4+, V3+, and V2+, respectively. The V 2p spectrum of L-V2CTx-MXene revealed that the peak intensities of V2+ and V3+ are much higher than that of V4+, which is consistent with previous reports (Zhao et al., 2023). This suggests that the vanadium atoms in L-V2CTx-MXene retain a range of valence states, which contributes to its good electrocatalytic performance. Then analyzing the V 2p spectrum of CQDs@MXene reveals significant changes in the V valence states following the incorporation of CQDs. Specifically, the V4+ peak area ratio is noticeably higher whereas the V2+ and V3+ peak area ratios are declining, indicating a shift towards a higher vanadium valence state. After selenization, the V4+ peak intensity becomes dominant, although peaks corresponding to V2+ and V3+ are still present, with their peak area ratio increasing (Chia et al., 2016). These observations suggest that high-valent vanadium species are partially reduced by the CQDs during the high-temperature selenization of CQDs@MXene. Additionally, these results agree with the XRD observations.

Fig. 2h presents a high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1 s, showing three peaks at 529.52, 531.33, and 533.06 eV, which correspond to VO, V-C-OH, and H-O-H. The intensity of the VO peak in the V 2p spectrum of CQDs@MXene increases dramatically, indicating that VO dominates the functional groups on the surface of V2CTx-MXene after the hydrothermal intercalation reaction of CQDs. In addition, the positive shift of the VO peak aligns with the elevated V valence state (Zhang, Ma, et al., 2023). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the C 1 s (Fig. S3a) reveals the presence of the V − C (282.57 eV), C − C (284.8 eV), C − OH (286.57 eV), and O − C=O (288.74 eV) bonds. A comparative analysis of the XPS spectra for CV bonds in L-V2CTx MXene and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene demonstrates that selenium treatment leads to a reduction in CV bonds while simultaneously increasing the concentration of O-C=O bonds. This observation implies that significant selenium doping contributes to the cleavage of CV bonds. Additionally, a portion of the -OH groups is transformed into hydrogen and O-C=O bonds due to selenium treatment. The reduction of -OH groups enhance the redox reactivity of the surface detectors, thereby significantly improving the electrochemical sensing performance (Jiang et al., 2022). The Se 3d spectrum of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene (Fig. S3b) shows a Se2− peak at 54.15/53.35 eV and two additional peaks at 55.94/54.89 eV, which correspond to Se 3d3/2 and 3d2/5 orbitals, labeled as Se0. The appearance of the Se0 peak is attributed to slight selenium vapor condensation on the surface of the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene during the cooling process (Sha et al., 2022).

Fig. 3a displays the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of bare GCE, L-V2CTx MXene/GCE, CQDs@MXene/GCE, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE. Compared to bare GCE, L-V2CTx MXene/GCE, and CQDs@MXene/GCE, the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE exhibits much higher peak currents. This enhancement suggests that the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene composite, formed through selenization, expedites the transport of electrons by creating a more conducive route between the electrode and the electrochemical probe [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to assess the electrode's capacity for electron transport (Fig. 3b). The charge-transfer resistance (Rct) of different electrodes was assessed by analyzing EIS data using an equivalent circuit model. In the equivalent circuit (Fig. 3c), Rs represents the ohmic resistance of the electrolyte solution, Rct represents the electron transfer resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface, Zw represents the diffusion impedance, and CPE represents the constant phase element. The Rct values of bare/GCE, L-V2CTx MXene/GCE, CQDs@MXene/GCE, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE were 209 Ω, 162 Ω, 49.9 Ω, and 15.9 Ω, respectively. The heterogeneous electron transfer rate constants (k0) were calculated using the equation (Adane et al., 2023; Jiang et al., 2024):
(1)
k o
=
RT
R ct
AC
F 2

Where R, T, n, F, C, A, and k0 are the gas constant, temperature, the electron transfer number, the Faraday constant, the solution concentration, the electrode surface area, and the standard heterogeneous rate constant, respectively. The calculated k0 values were 3.06 × 10−3 cm s−1 for bare GCE, 4.62 × 10−3 cm s−1 for L- V2CTx MXene/GCE, 15.12 × 10−3 cm s−1 for CQDs@MXene/GCE, and 52.65 × 10−3 cm s−1 for VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE. The VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE exhibited the lowest Rct and highest k0, reflecting superior kinetics and excellent electronic conductivity.

CV conducted at various scan rates can be utilized to assess the effective electrochemically active surface area (EASA) of the modified electrode materials. Fig. 3(d-f) shows the CV curves of L-V2CTx MXene/GCE, CQDs@MXene/GCE, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE. Fig. 3g illustrates the linear correlation between the square root of the scan rate and anodic peak currents. The Randles-Sevick equation was utilized to compute the EASA for sensors (Mishra et al., 2023; Zhang, Han, Sun, Li, Li, & Gui, 2024):
(2)
I ap
=
2.69 ×
10 5
n
3 2
A
D
1 2
c
v
1 2

Where c is the solution concentration, v is the scan rate, A is the EASA, D is the diffusion coefficient, and n is the number of electrons transferred. The calculated EASA of the L-V2CTx MXene/GCE, CQDs@MXene/GCE, and VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE were 0.0772 cm2, 0.0915 cm2, and 0.1149 cm2, respectively. Apparently, VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE showed the largest EASA, implying the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE modified layer has more active sites than usual.

The electrocatalytic performance of various electrodes for HQ and CC were studied in Fig. 3h. The bare GCE displayed a modest current response, demonstrating its poor electrocatalytic activity for both substances. The L-V2CTx MXene/GCE displayed two pairs of weak redox peaks, in contrast, CQDs@MXene/GCE exhibited a more distinct separation of redox peak and a significant enhancement in peak currents. This is attributed to the introduction of CQDs into the L-V2CTx MXene interlayer, which increases both the active surface area and the availability of active sites the target analytes. As expected, the VSe2@V2O3 @V2CTx MXene/GCE exhibited the highest current response for electrochemical sensing of the two analytes. Compared to CQDs@MXene/GCE, the oxidation peak currents of HQ and CC were 2.37 and 2.49 times higher, respectively. This indicates that VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE offers superior electrocatalytic activity. The remarkable performance of this electrode can be ascribed to several factors. Firstly, VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene provides excellent conductivity and possesses a large active surface area. Secondly, the synergistic effect of the VSe2, V2O3, and V2CTx MXene components enhances the redox reaction of the target analytes. Lastly, the VSe2 nanosheets on the surface facilitate rapid ion diffusion.

Fig. 3i presents a comprehensive analysis of the electrochemical sensing capabilities of various electrodes modified with different components. It is evident that VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene outperforms other materials, exhibiting the lowest electrochemical impedance, the highest heterogeneous electron transfer rate constants, the largest electrochemical active surface area, and the highest response current for HQ and CC. These results indicate that the CQDs intercalated L-V2CTx MXene selenization strategy can effectively improve the performance of electrochemical sensing for HQ and CC. Furthermore, VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE proves to be a promising non-enzymatic sensor for the simultaneous detection of HQ and CC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions and electrochemical oxidation kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the optimum performance, several factors of the electrochemical sensor were optimized, including the concentration of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene applied dropwise, the volume of the droplets, and the pH of the electrolyte solution. Fig. S4a and S4b show the DPV curves for VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene concentrations ranging from 1.0 to 3.0 mg mL−1 and the corresponding linear relationship between response current and concentration. The data show that response currents diminish when the concentration deviates from 2 mg mL−1. Thus, the concentration of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene was optimized at 2 mg mL−1. The effects of various dripping amounts on the electrochemical sensor were investigated (Fig. S4c and S4d). The results indicate that response currents for HQ and CC increased significantly with the increase of the amount of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene suspension in the range of 6–10 μL. However, the response currents intensity decreased after increasing from 10 μL to 14 μL, so the optimal dosage of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene suspension was 10 μL. The observed increase in response current with higher concentrations or volumes of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene can be attributed to the increase in active sites on the electrode surface. Conversely, the decline in response current at higher volumes is likely due to the formation of an excessively thick modification layer, which impairs electron transport and reduces mass transfer efficiency (Niu et al., 2024).

A critical factor influencing the performance of electrochemical sensors is the pH of the buffer solution. As shown in Fig. 4 (a-c), the electrocatalytic activity of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE for 100 μM HQ and 100 μM CC were investigated in 0.10 M PBS across a pH range of 6.0 to 8.0. The peak currents for both HQ and CC initially increased with pH but subsequently decreased beyond a certain point, with the maximum response current recorded at pH 7.0. Consequently, 0.1 M PBS at pH 7.0 was chosen for further experimentation. Moreover, a strong linear relationship was established between anodic peak potential (Epa) and pH, with the following linear equations: Epa (V) = 0.469–0.058 pH (R2 = 0.997) for HQ and Epa (V) = 0.575–0.059 pH (R2 = 0.999) for CC. The slopes for both HQ and CC were found to be comparable to the theoretical value of 0.059 V pH −1, suggesting the simultaneous participation of protons and electrons in the electrode reaction (Joseph et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2024).

To investigate the electrochemical redox behaviors of 100 μM HQ and 100 μM CC, CV measurements were performed in mixed solutions at varying scan rates. As illustrated in Fig. 4d, the current responses for both HQ and CC exhibited an increase with the elevation of the scan rate. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 4e, the redox peak current demonstrated a proportional relationship to the square root of the scan rate, suggesting that the redox reactions of CC and HQ on the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE are diffusion-controlled (Adane et al., 2024). Furthermore, Fig. 4f shows that the oxidation peak potential (Epa) exhibited a slight increase with the elevation of the scan rate, and Epa demonstrated a linear correlation with the natural logarithm of the scan rate (lnv). The linear regression equations established for Epa in relation to lnv are as follows: Epa = 0.024 lnv + 0.025 (R 2 = 0.999) for HQ and Epa = 0.023 lnv + 0.131 (R 2 = 0.999) for CC. Utilizing the linear regression equation that relates Epa to lnv, the number of electrons (n) involved in the oxidation reaction observed on the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE surface can be determined using the Laviron equation (Laviron, 1979; Zhang, Han, Sun, & Li, 2024):
(3)
2.303 × slope =
1.15 RT
αF
(4)

=
E 0
+
RT
1 − α
nF
lnv

Where R represents gas constant (R = 8.3145 J/mol/K), T represents room temperature (T = 298 K), F represents Faraday's constant (F = 96,485C/mol), α is the transfer coefficient, Epa is the standard potential. By calculation, n = 2.31 for HQ and n = 2.25 for CC, both approximately equal to 2. These findings, combined with the pH-dependent experiments, suggest that the electrooxidation of HQ and CC at the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE electrode follows a two-proton and two-electron transfer process. The proposed redox reactions of HQ and CC are illustrated and shown in Fig. 4g and Fig. 4h (Xu et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Individual and simultaneous determination of HQ and CC
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the optimal conditions described above, HQ and CC were quantitatively analyzed using a VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE modified GCE. Fig. 5a shows the results of DPV tests in PBS (pH = 7.0) containing 50 μM CC and varying HQ concentrations from 0.5 to 600 μM. The oxidation peak current of HQ increased with increasing concentration, while the peak current of CC remained nearly unchanged at 50 μM. Similarly, Fig. 5b shows that the DPV signal for CC increased with concentration between 0.5 and 600 μM, while the peak current for HQ remained relatively constant. Calibration curves correlating HQ and CC concentrations with DPV response currents are shown in Fig. 5d and 5e. For HQ, the calibration equations are Ipa = 0.111CHQ + 2.410 (R2 = 0.989) and Ipa = 0.049CHQ + 8.628 (R2 = 0.991) for 0.5–100 μM and 100–600 μM, respectively. For CC, in the low concentration range (0.5–100 μM), the calibration equations are Ipa = 0.118CCC + 2.861 (R2 = 0.994), and in the high concentration range (100–600 μM) is Ipa = 0.038CCC + 10.373 (R2 = 0.997). The calculated limit of detection (LOD) of HQ and CC is 0.231 μM and 0.225 μM respectively, with a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3. Additionally, we employed DPV to detect varying concentrations of HQ and CC simultaneously. The oxidation peak currents obtained exhibited a linear correlation with the concentrations of HQ and CC, ranging from 0.5 to 600 μM (Fig. 5c), with the linear regression equations detailed in Fig. 5 f. For HQ, the linear regression equations are Ipa = 0.151CHQ + 3.970 (0.5–100 μM, R2 = 0.984) and Ipa = 0.037CHQ + 15.081 (100–600 μM, R2 = 0.993). For CC, the equations are Ipa = 0.171CCC + 4.591 (0.5–100 μM, R2 = 0.976) and Ipa = 0.041CCC + 16.476 (100–600 μM, R2 = 0.994). The LOD for HQ and CC were 0.144 μM and 0.131 μM, respectively (S/N = 3). These results demonstrate that the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE electrode-based electrochemical sensors can effectively differentiate and simultaneously detect HQ and CC isomers in mixed solution environments.

In addition, VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE exhibited a wider linear range and lower limit of detection compared to other reported sensors. The linear range is improved by at least one order of magnitude compared to previous works as shown in Fig. 5 g and Table S1. The limit of detection (LOD) is reduced by one order of magnitude compared to Poly DY11/Zn/CuO (Sumanth et al., 2023), N,Si-GQDs (Lei, Zhan et al., 2024) and Ce-MOF(TV)/CNTs (Huang, Chen et al., 2021). The superior performance of the developed sensor can be attributed to the large active surface area of the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene composite, which facilitates exposure more active sites. The heterostructure design of VSe₂@V₂O₃@V₂CTx facilitates favorable electronic interactions between the components (Ma et al., 2024). VSe₂, with metallic conductivity, serves as an electron conduit, reducing charge transfer resistance (Sha et al., 2022). Meanwhile, the VSe₂@V₂O₃@V₂CTx MXene structure contains abundant redox couples (e.g., V4+/V3+ and V3+/V2+) that provide more active sites for HQ and CC, thereby enhancing adsorption capacity and improving sensitivity. Additionally, the rich redox-active species promote electron transfer and accelerate the reaction kinetics. Thus, this synergistic effect significantly enhances electrocatalytic performance (Liu et al., 2023; Tan et al., 2022).

To access the selectivity of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE, we analyzed the peak currents of 100 μM HQ and CC in the presence of 10-fold concentrations of potential interferents, including Na+, K+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, I−, NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2−, Br−, ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), resorcin (RS), and phenol (pH) (Fig. 6a and b). There is no significant change in the current response of HQ and CC with the addition of interferents, indicating that the prepared sensor possesses a satisfactory selectivity in complex matrices. The main reason is that the modified electrode does not generate current responses to Na+, K+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, I−, NO3 −, Cl−, SO4 2−, Br−, AA, Glu (Fig. S5a), while the oxidation peak potentials of the response currents generated by RS and Ph are 0.57 V and 0.64 V (Fig. S5b), which are much higher than the oxidation peak potentials of HQ and CC, and are not within the potential range for detecting HQ and CC by this sensor (−0.1–0.4 V). This is due to the differences in electronic structures between RS, pH, HQ, and CC, leading to different electron transport rates, as well as differences in the stability of their oxidation products, resulting in variations in catalytic oxidation potentials (Enache & Oliveira-Brett, 2011; Ngamchuea et al., 2020). Therefore, this modified electrode has good selectivity against these interferents.

The reproducibility, repeatability, and stability of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE were assessed by DPV. As illustrated in Fig. S6a, five parallel VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE were tested in 0.1 M PBS containing 100 μM HQ and 100 μM CC. The current response variations are presented in Fig. 6c, with relative standard deviations (RSD) of 2.95 % for HQ and 1.76 % for CC. In addition, as shown in Fig. S6b and Fig. 6d, five replicates of the same VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE was evaluated for100 μM HQ and 100 μM CC with RSD of 1.64 % and 1.23 %, respectively. The stability of the modified electrodes was assessed by subjecting them to ambient storage at room temperature for a duration of several days, with evaluations conducted every three days utilizing DPV. After 15 days of ambient storage, the current signals of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE were 89.84 % and 89.39 % of their initial values for HQ and CC, respectively (Fig. 6e). This analysis demonstrates that the VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE had acceptable reproducibility, repeatability, and stability.

To assess the practical application of the modified electrode, the concentration of HQ and CC solutions to drinking water and beverages was analyzed using VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene/GCE. The analysis of real water samples was conducted using the standard addition method, with each concentration (10, 50, and 100 μM) tested three times in parallel. As presented in Table S2, the recovery rates for HQ ranged from 98.96 % to 103.16 %, while CC recovery rates varied from 94.33 % to 101.51 %. Moreover, their RSD was below 5 %. These results demonstrate the significant potential of VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene-based electrochemical sensors for simultaneously determining HQ and CC in real aqueous environments.

In summary, in this study, VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene nanocomposites were successfully synthesized using few-layer V2CTx MXene as a precursor and CQDs as a protective intercalation agent. This approach enabled the in-situ chemical growth of VSe2 nanosheets and V2O3 on the surface of V2CTx MXene through a gas-phase pyrolysis selenization strategy. The resulting VSe2@V2O3@V2CTx MXene exhibits good conductivity and a large active surface area. Furthermore, the in-situ grown VSe2 nanosheets demonstrate strong adsorption capabilities for both HQ and CC, resulting in remarkable sensing performance for these compounds. For simultaneous detection of HQ and CC isomers, the linear ranges are both 0.5–600 μM, with LOD at 0.144 μM for HQ and 0.131 μM for CC (S/N = 3). Notably, there is almost no interference between HQ and CC when simultaneously detecting HQ and CC. The sensor was also tested with real water samples, yielding satisfactory results. This work presents a new strategy for enhancing the electrochemical activity of V2CTx MXene while highlighting its potential applications in the field of food safety monitoring.

Yu Han: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Li Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Shaobin Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Fengbo Li: Resources. Jianjiao Xin: Visualization. Xue Wang: Methodology. Minjie Liang: Investigation.
